text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

25.1	The Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) is responsible for leading and coordinating international action for the protection of refugees and the search for solutions to their plight. Its mandate derives from relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 319 A (IV), by which the Assembly established the Office, and 428 (V), which sets out its statute. In addition, the international legal basis for the protection of refugees finds its principal expression in the Convention relating to the Status of Refugees of 1951 and the Protocol relating to the Status of Refugees of 1967. UNHCR also has responsibilities for stateless persons pursuant to paragraph 6 (A) (ii) of its statute and article I (A) (2) of the 1951 Convention with regard to refugees who are stateless, as well as to various Assembly resolutions in relation to the Convention relating to the Status of Stateless Persons of 1954 and the Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness of 1961. The mandate of UNHCR has been extended to other groups through various Assembly resolutions. UNHCR provides protection and assistance to internally displaced persons, working in cooperation with the Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator and in the context of the collaborative response of the United Nations system, as well as with the consent of the State concerned.
Programme of work
Objective

25.2	The objective, to which this programme contributes, is to ensure international protection for refugees and other persons of concern and to achieve durable solutions to their plight, in cooperation with States and other organizations, without discrimination.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

25.3	To contribute to the objective, UNHCR will, in close cooperation with national and local authorities, partners in the United Nations system, non-governmental organizations, persons of concern, the private sector, development partners, academia and other relevant stakeholders:

(a)	Swiftly and effectively respond to humanitarian crises, including in conflict-affected areas, by providing protection, humanitarian and life-saving assistance, while ensuring preparedness measures to respond to new emergencies with agility;
(b)	Advocate for accession and adherence by States to relevant international and regional instruments, including the refugee and statelessness conventions;
(c) 	Promote and facilitate the admission, reception and treatment of refugees in accordance with internationally agreed standards and advocate for the prevention and reduction of statelessness;

(d)	Support implementation of the Global Compact on Refugees, follow up on the implementation of pledges from the Global Refugee Forums, advocate for more active international solidarity and equitable burden- and responsibility-sharing to support communities and countries hosting refugees, and promote progress towards sustainable responses;
(e)	Enhance the protection and assistance for internally displaced persons under the Inter-Agency Standing Committee coordination system, in line with relevant General Assembly resolutions;

(f)	Contribute to the resilience of forcibly displaced persons and their host communities faced with the adverse effects of climate change and disasters, in consultation with national authorities and in cooperation with competent agencies.

25.4	By promoting comprehensive approaches, UNHCR will contribute to relevant goals contained in the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, ensuring that refugees and other persons of concern are not left behind in development processes (see A/AC.96/76/5).
25.5	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Significant advances in the response to refugee situations through more predictable and equitable burden- and responsibility-sharing and through a multi-stakeholder approach, consistent with the Global Compact on Refugees;
(b)	Strengthened national asylum systems that are compliant with international legal standards;

(c)	The advancement of solutions for forcibly displaced people, including voluntary repatriation and, where appropriate and feasible, local integration and resettlement, while reaffirming the importance of support to facilitate sustainable reintegration in countries of origin;
(d)	Reinforced solutions for stateless people, resulting in previously stateless individuals acquiring nationality or having their nationality confirmed;
(e)	Improved access to protection and assistance for refugees and asylum-seekers through registration.
25.6	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Security conditions shall allow UNHCR to carry out its programmes;
(b)	Humanitarian access will be granted in most countries, enabling UNHCR to respond to urgent protection and assistance needs;

(c)	States and the international community at large will remain committed to contributing to and supporting the implementation of the Global Compact on Refugees, including pledges made at the Global Refugee Forum;
(d)	Asylum space and policies will not further restrict admissions in some countries;
(e)	Voluntary funding from donors will match the budgeted needs to respond effectively and adequately to situations of forced displacement and statelessness.

25.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, UNHCR will engage a wide range of partners in programme delivery. UNHCR will strengthen partnerships with local actors, regional organizations, organizations led by refugees and other persons of concern, development actors and international financial institutions to advance the inclusion of displaced populations in United Nations programmes and national humanitarian and development policies and plans. UNHCR will strengthen feedback and response mechanisms as a means of consulting displaced and stateless people on its programmes. The Office will continue to invest in private sector partnerships in order to secure support for strategic and long-term responses. Efforts to address protection needs by empowering affected communities and fostering their resilience and self-reliance until durable solutions are found will be a priority.

25.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UNHCR will cooperate closely with resident and humanitarian coordinators to promote overarching comprehensive protection and solutions strategies. UNHCR will coordinate with other agencies by leveraging comparative advantages in support of nationally defined priorities and planning frameworks. This approach enhances complementarity and collective outcomes. The Office will remain engaged in United Nations coordination forums, including the Inter-Agency Standing Committee and the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination. Building on its institutional plan on solutions to internal displacement, UNHCR will continue to engage in situations of internal displacement.

25.9	UNHCR integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. UNHCR will remain fully committed to a non-discriminatory approach, ensuring that its responses and interventions consider the needs, priorities and capacities of affected communities. It will work to prevent, mitigate and respond to gender-based violence and violence against children, including sexual violence. The Office will also enhance collaboration with organizations led by forcibly displaced and stateless persons, including those led by women.

25.10	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, UNHCR will enhance the participation of persons with disabilities and strengthen partnerships with organizations of persons with disabilities and the Global Network of Refugees with Disabilities. UNHCR will promote non-discrimination and disability inclusion across its programmes and will advocate for equal access for forcibly displaced persons with disabilities to social protection schemes.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

25.11	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative.

25.12	UNHCR will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals relating to the realignment of responsibilities and the reduction in programmatic overlaps. UNHCR is working closely with other entities to clarify roles and responsibilities and establish clearer divisions of labour to enable greater efficiency, predictability and results at scale.
Evaluation activities
25.13 	The following evaluations conducted by UNHCR and completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation of gender-based violence prevention, risk mitigation and response;
(b)	Evaluation of the UNHCR multi-country emergency response to the situation in the Sudan;
(c)	Evaluation of the Senegal multi-country office strategy;
(d)	Country strategy evaluations in Brazil, Bulgaria, the Republic of Korea and Somalia.

25.14	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, UNHCR will strengthen partnerships with local actors across operational contexts to more effectively prevent and respond to gender-based violence. The design of future emergency responses will be enhanced by capitalizing on cross-regional approaches, including for contingency planning, cross-border operations and regional communication plans. The country strategy and multi-country office strategy evaluations will inform UNHCR strategies for 2027 in the relevant regions.
25.15	The following evaluations to be conducted by UNHCR are planned for 2027:
(a)	Thematic evaluation on economic inclusion and self-reliance of refugees in urban areas;
(b)	Evaluation of the UNHCR policy on cash-based interventions;
(c)	Evaluation of the UNHCR approach to strengthening partnerships with local actors;
(d)	Country and multi-country office strategy evaluations.
Programme performance in 2025
Global Refugee Forum pledges advance responsibility-sharing and support for refugees and host communities

25.16	As a framework for predictable and equitable responsibility-sharing, the Global Compact on Refugees is intended to transform how the world responds to refugee situations, benefiting both refugees and host communities. In December 2025, the progress review of the Global Refugee Forum assessed progress made towards achieving the Global Compacts’ objectives, reviewed the implementation and achievement of pledges and identified areas for renewed attention and support. UNHCR plays a catalytical role to ensure that the pledges benefit directly refugees, hosting States and countries of origin, and the cities and communities that support them. More than 60 new policy and financial pledges were announced, with significant commitments from States and the private sector. Together, these commitments have delivered measurable impact for refugees and communities that host them, with inclusive policies strengthening both individual self-reliance and national economies. These approaches have expanded access to decent work, increased incomes and public revenue and improved access to essential services such as health and education. For example, by 2025, legal access to the labour market enabled approximately 33 million refugees in 96 countries to pursue livelihoods and contribute to their host communities. At the community level, fulfilled pledges also fostered social inclusion and well-being, with 336,000 refugees and host community members reached through sports and other community activities that promote connection, resilience and shared opportunity.
25.17	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 25.1)
Figure 25.I
Performance measure: number of Global Refugee Forum pledges (cumulative)
Result 1: increased prevalence of national and local partners in refugee response plans globally – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
25.18	The programme’s work contributed to 35 per cent of local and national operational partners being involved in refugee response plans globally, which exceeded the planned target of 34 per cent.
25.19	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 25.II).
Figure 25.II
Performance measure: percentage of local and national operational partners in refugee response plans globally (as a proportion of all partners)
Result 2: strengthened protection and access to services for refugees and asylum-seekers – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
25.20	The programme’s work contributed to 412,000 persons of concern who created digital accounts through the Digital Gateway, which exceeded the planned target of 400,000.
25.21	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 25.III).
Figure 25.III
Performance measure: number of persons of concern who have created digital accounts through the Digital Gateway (cumulative)
Result 3: increased adherence to international conventions on statelessness – Proposed programme plan for 2027

25.22	Statelessness remains a critical global issue, given that the absence of nationality and legal identity continues to expose individuals to long-term exclusion and vulnerability. UNHCR estimates that some 4.4 million people worldwide are stateless or of undetermined nationality, with millions more unrecorded in official data. In line with the UNHCR mandate and guided by the 1954 Convention relating to the Status of Stateless Persons and the 1961 Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness, UNHCR supports Governments in identifying and protecting stateless persons and preventing and resolving statelessness.
Lessons learned and planned change

25.23	The lesson for UNHCR was that progress in addressing statelessness was most significant when legal and policy reforms were accompanied by firm government support and sustained national engagement. While legislative reforms have advanced in several contexts, continued technical support is required for effective implementation. In applying the lesson, UNHCR will prioritize advocacy for solutions and accession to the conventions and will strengthen national capacities and share best practices to support the implementation of international legal standards. Sustained engagement at the national level will be reinforced through multi-stakeholder collaboration under the Global Alliance to End Statelessness.
25.24	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 25.IV)
Figure 25.IV
Performance measure: number of States that are signatories to the 1954 Convention relating to the Status of Stateless Persons and the 1961 Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness (cumulative)
Legislative mandates
25.25	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the Office.

C.	Substantive deliverables

Consultation, advice and advocacy: advice on refugee protection and solutions and on the rights of refugees, other forcibly displaced persons and stateless persons under international, regional and national law, to approximately 109 countries; and support for the legislative processes of 64 countries; dialogue and consultations with partners.
